place.name: Hull Old Town and Docks Core
place.summary: A dense historic core where estuary geography, royal foundation, medieval fortification, abolition, dock engineering, fishing, bombing and civic reinvention all stack tightly together.

entries.title: The River Hull Meets the Humber and Creates a Natural Port Core
entries.description: The junction of the River Hull and the Humber estuary created a natural transfer point between inland Yorkshire and maritime routes. This confluence gave the old town site its enduring strategic and commercial importance.
entries.significance: This is the geographical reason Hull’s historic core exists where it does.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the lower River Hull and old harbour zone beside the old town.

entries.title: Wyke upon Hull Develops at the River-Port Site
entries.description: Before the royal borough existed, Wyke upon Hull occupied the strategically useful ground near the river mouth. It was a modest but well-positioned trading settlement that prefigured the city to come.
entries.significance: This marks the local beginning of the urban site that became Hull.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the old town core.

entries.title: Edward I Founds Kingston upon Hull
entries.description: Edward I purchased Wyke and re-founded it as Kingston upon Hull in 1299, creating a royal borough at a strategically valuable port site on the Humber.
entries.significance: This is the formal birth of Hull as a royal town.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the medieval old town core.

entries.title: Hull’s Medieval Port Grows Around the Old Harbour
entries.description: The medieval port expanded around the old harbour and river frontage, handling wool exports and continental trade. Merchants, quays and warehouses helped turn the town into one of England’s important ports.
entries.significance: This is where Hull’s commercial maritime identity became structurally embedded.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the old harbour and merchant quarter.

entries.title: Beverley Gate Forms Part of Hull’s Defended Edge
entries.description: Hull’s walls, ditches and gates enclosed the medieval town, with Beverley Gate serving as one of its principal entrances. The gate later became famous as the point where national political drama played out in 1642.
entries.significance: It connected the physical fortification of the town to later Civil War history.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the west side of the old town near the known Beverley Gate site.

entries.title: Hull Refuses Entry to Charles I at Beverley Gate
entries.description: Sir John Hotham refused King Charles I entry to Hull in April 1642, denying him access to the town’s arsenal. The confrontation became one of the opening acts of the English Civil War.
entries.significance: Few city gates in Britain can claim to have helped tip a kingdom into war.

entries.title: Hull Holds Out During the First Civil War Siege
entries.description: Royalist forces besieged Hull in 1642, hoping to seize its strategic stores and port. The town resisted, preserving its role as a key Parliamentarian stronghold.
entries.significance: The fortified old town became a nationally significant military holdfast.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the fortified town core and defensive perimeter.

entries.title: William Wilberforce Is Born in the Old Town
entries.description: William Wilberforce was born in Hull’s old town in 1759. His later parliamentary leadership in the campaign against the slave trade made this part of Hull globally significant in the history of abolition.
entries.significance: This links the old town directly to one of the great moral and political transformations of the British Empire.
entries.area_note: Anchored to Wilberforce House in the museum quarter of the old town.

entries.title: The Old Dock Opens Near the Historic Centre
entries.description: Hull’s Old Dock opened in 1778 and is widely regarded as Britain’s first commercial wet dock. It represented a major leap in dock engineering and transformed the relationship between the town centre and maritime trade.
entries.significance: It made Hull a pioneer in the infrastructure of modern port cities.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the site now occupied by Queen’s Gardens.

entries.title: Queen’s Gardens Occupies the Former Old Dock Basin
entries.description: The former Old Dock basin was later transformed into the sunken green space now known as Queen’s Gardens. Few city parks more neatly conceal an earlier life as industrial port infrastructure.
entries.significance: This is a rare and elegant example of dock engineering transformed into civic landscape.

entries.title: Fruit Market and Waterfront Warehousing Define the Dock Edge
entries.description: Warehouses, merchants and market activity along the waterfront and Fruit Market zone embodied the trading life of dockside Hull. Goods, labour and shipping all converged here in dense urban proximity.
entries.significance: This district shows how tightly port activity and urban life were once interwoven in central Hull.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the Fruit Market and waterfront edge south-east of the old town.

entries.title: Hull’s Fishing Economy Radiates Inward from the Docks
entries.description: Although the great fishing dock infrastructure stretched beyond the old town, the fish trade shaped the central city through labour, transport, markets and maritime identity. Hull’s urban core carried the rhythms of a global fishing port.
entries.significance: The old town and dockside centre cannot be understood apart from the fishing economy that animated them.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the inner dock and waterfront interface between old town and maritime industry.

entries.title: Bombing Devastates the Old Town and Waterfront
entries.description: The old town, docklands and waterfront suffered severe damage during the Second World War as Hull was repeatedly bombed because of its strategic significance. Much of the historic urban fabric was scarred or destroyed.
entries.significance: Modern Hull’s centre was shaped as much by wartime loss as by medieval survival.
entries.area_note: Anchored broadly to the old town and adjacent dockside districts.

entries.title: The Deep Reimagines the River-Estuary Edge
entries.description: The Deep opened at Sammy’s Point near the confluence of the River Hull and the Humber, turning a once purely industrial river edge into a site of scientific display, public learning and striking contemporary architecture.
entries.significance: It shows the historic port core reinventing itself without abandoning its waterside identity.
entries.area_note: Directly anchored to The Deep at the edge of the old port landscape.

entries.title: City of Culture Reanimates Hull’s Historic Centre
entries.description: Hull’s year as UK City of Culture brought installations, performances and civic energy into the old town, museums quarter and waterfront. Historic streets became stages for a renewed cultural self-image.
entries.significance: The old town shifted from being merely historic backdrop to active cultural engine.
entries.area_note: Anchored to the old town and city-centre event spaces.

metadata.notes: Hull micro-pack focused on the Old Town, waterfront and dock-engineering core. Designed to complement the wider Kingston upon Hull super pack with a denser, walkable historic nucleus.
